coredata.dc:title: ROS scavenging carbon dots chelated hydroxyapatite filler: Target synthesis and enhancing adhesion of hydrogel
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Guanxiong
Li, Baoqiang
Liu, Zonglin
Dong, Jiaxin
Kang, Xuefeng
Feng, Yujie
Jia, Dechang
Zhou, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots/hydroxyapatite filler could have the reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging ability of CDs and the mechanical property improvement ability of hydroxyapatite. Carbon dots (CDs) not only have optical fluorescence by its physical property, but also regulates ROS by its biological property and had the binding ability of calcium ions by its chemical property. Based on the chelation ability and ROS regulation ability of CDs, ROS scavenging carbon dots chelated hydroxyapatite filler (ROS-HA) was target synthesized via chelating ability and the synthetic yield was 70.9%. The chelation sites of CDs were 2.89 ± 0.02 and after chelated hydroxyapatite, the chelation sites of ROS-HA were 0.69 ± 0.02. The maximum hydroxyl radical scavenging rate and superoxide radical scavenging rate of ROS-HA were 72% and 60%, respectively. ROS-HA improved 86% the adhesion performance of hydrogel-based composites compared to the hydrogel. ROS-HA provided new filler for construction the ROS regulation, tissue adhesive and fluorescent hydrogel application in wound dressing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogel-based composites
Carbon dots/hydroxyapatite filler
Reactive oxygen species
Chelating

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optics and surface property of ROS-HA
Coordination constant and chelation sites of ROS-CDs and ROS-HA
Selectivity and sensitivity of Ca2+ detection by fluorescence
ROS-HA enhancing hydrogel strength, modulus and adhesion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High-yield synthesis and surface property of ROS-HA. (a) Schematic presentation of synthetic process. (b) Synthetic yield of ROS-HA by different solvent. (c) Synthetic pH of ROS-HA derived from different solvent. (d) UV-vis spectra. (e) Photoluminescence spectra. (f) XRD pattern of ROS-HA and HA-CS composite. (g) FTIR spectrum. (h) XPS C1s spectrum, (i) XPS N1s spectrum.

HR-TEM micrographs, chelation constant and chelation sites of ROS-HA： (a) 100 nm (b) 40 nm (c) 10 nm (d) 2 nm. Coordination constant K of (e) ROS-CDs, (f) ROS-HA and (g) comparation of coordination constant K. Chelation sites N of (h) ROS-CDs, (i) ROS-HA and (j) comparation of chelation sites N.

Selectivity and sensitivity of Ca2+ detection by chelating ability and fluorescence. (a) Selective of ROS-HA with different ions. (b) Recognition of calcium ions by ROS-HA under ion interference. (c) Fluorescence spectra of ROS-HA with different concentrations of calcium ions. (d) Linear relationship between ROS-HA concentration and relative fluorescence intensity. (e) Mechanism diagram of calcium ion detection by ROS-HA using chelation ability.

The ability of ROS-HA scavenging different free radicals: (a) Curve of ROS-HA scavenging hydroxyl radicals with different concentrations, (b) IC50. (c) Curve of ROS-HA scavenging superoxide radical with different concentrations, (d) IC50. DPPH Radical Scavenging: (e) Clearance curves at different concentrations of ROS-HA. (f) DPPH Radical Scavenging IC50 value of ROS-HA. Cyclic voltammograms of Fe3+ mixed with (g) HA and CDs and with (h) different concentrations of ROS-HA at scan rate of 20 mV/s（i) Absorption of BSA.

ROS-HA enhancing hydrogel strength, modulus and adhesion. (a) Schematic diagram and fluorescence photograph of ROS-HA hydrogel. (b) SEM image. (c)Elemental mapping analysis of Ca. (d) Merged SEM image with Ca distribution maps. (e)Elemental mapping analysis of P. (f) Tensile stress at break, (g) Young's modulus, (h) adhesion energy, (i) max tensile strain, (j) swelling ratio of the ROS-HA hydrogels.

head.ce:title: ROS scavenging carbon dots chelated hydroxyapatite filler: Target synthesis and enhancing adhesion of hydrogel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots/hydroxyapatite filler could have the reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging ability of CDs and the mechanical property improvement ability of hydroxyapatite. Carbon dots (CDs) not only have optical fluorescence by its physical property, but also regulates ROS by its biological property and had the binding ability of calcium ions by its chemical property. Based on the chelation ability and ROS regulation ability of CDs, ROS scavenging carbon dots chelated hydroxyapatite filler (ROS-HA) was target synthesized via chelating ability and the synthetic yield was 70.9%. The chelation sites of CDs were 2.89 ± 0.02 and after chelated hydroxyapatite, the chelation sites of ROS-HA were 0.69 ± 0.02. The maximum hydroxyl radical scavenging rate and superoxide radical scavenging rate of ROS-HA were 72% and 60%, respectively. ROS-HA improved 86% the adhesion performance of hydrogel-based composites compared to the hydrogel. ROS-HA provided new filler for construction the ROS regulation, tissue adhesive and fluorescent hydrogel application in wound dressing.

ROS scavenging carbon dots chelated hydroxyapatite filler was target synthesized via chelating ability. Carbon dots with scavenging of reactive oxygen species were binding to the surface of hydroxyapatite, and the filler enhanced adhesion of hydrogel.
Image 1

ce:keyword.ce:text: ROS scavenging carbon dots chelated hydroxyapatite filler

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) has been successfully used to treat different pathological diseases [1–3] of cancer diseases [4,5], neurodegenerative diseases [6–8] and bacterial infections [9,10]. Biological mineralization is an important process in human life [11–13]，hydroxyapatite (HA) had advantages of good biocompatibility [14–17], biological activity [18–20], non-toxicity [21,22] and ability to guide bone growth [23–25], etc., it has been widely used in bone repair replacement materials [26–28]. The preparation method of carbon dots (CDs) had made great progress and optimized the luminescence properties of CDs [29–32]. Importantly, CDs can regulate ROS in animal bone treatment area [33]. In order to give fluorescence visualization to biological materials, CDs and HA can be synthesized by covalent bonding and blending. EDC/NHS coupling technology was used to synthesize CDs/HA complexes with stable fluorescence performance [34]. However, this method requires multistep reaction. CDs/HA was also prepared by directly blending CDs with HA or spontaneously assembling CDs on the surface of HA, but the problem was fluorescence attenuation [35,36]. Current problems in CDs/HA was that the steps of CDs/HA based on covalent bonding were cumbersome, and combination evidence of the CDs/HA based on adsorption was not clear in the interface. At present, CDs/HA complexes can accelerate bone repair, improve stability and sensitivity of ion probes, and be used for cell imaging. However, they do not have the ability to control ROS, and most of the CDs/HA had unstable interface bonding. At the same time, the synthesis yield had not been studied, and process of combining CDs with HA was not clear. So, one-step synthesis of HA and CDs complex can reduce the problem of unclear interface combination. The combination of CDs and HA was also very important to study, meanwhile, chelation and coordination chemistry provided accurate methods.

CDs had chelating function and can chelate metal ions [37,38]. EDTA was used as a precursor to synthesize CDs with EDTA functional groups on the surface for Ca2+ ions selective detection [39]. So CDs not only have the ability to bind, but also have the ability of fluoresce in response to ions for CDs/HA. HA can enhance adhesion and modulus of hydrogels [40,41], and CDs can enhance mechanical properties of hydrogels [42–44]. Therefore, CDs and HA complexes can theoretically improve the strength, modulus and adhesion properties of hydrogels.

This work designed a ROS scavenging carbon dots chelated hydroxyapatite filler (ROS-HA) with high synthetic yield, chelating ability and fluorescence. CDs on the HA were assembled on the surface of HA using chelating ability. After CDs chelated the surface of HA, it still had ability of chelating calcium ions. The incorporation of ROS-HA improved the strength, modulus and adhesion properties of polyacrylic acid hydrogel composites. ROS-HA provided new filler for construction the ROS regulation, tissue adhesive and fluorescent hydrogel application in wound dressing.

As can be seen from Fig. 1 (a), ROS-HA with different yields can be targeting obtained by adding different substances to hydrogels containing HA. The process was hydrothermal carbonization and polymerization at a temperature of 220 °C to target high yield synthesis of ROS-HA. ROS-HA was synthesized based on the chelation of CDs, and the chelation ability of CDs and ROS-HA were shown in Fig. 2 . After CDs chelated the surface of HA, it still had the ability of chelating calcium ions (Fig. 3 ). These two chelation evidences were used to demonstrate chelation between CDs and HA. The resulting ROS-HA was sea cucumber-shaped and use chelating ability to link CDs to HA. ROS-HA not only had fluorescence property, but also had ROS scavenging ability from chitosan. From Fig. 1(b), it can be seen that using different precursors can targeting obtain different ROS-HA yields. The synthetic yield of ROS-HA using acrylamide/initiator/ammonia as the precursor was improved 0.21, 1.23 and 3.72 times higher than using solvent of acrylamide/ammonia, NaOH solution and deionized water, respectively. In Fig. 1(c), pH of the reaction solution was 4.04, 7.85, 7.50, 7.50 after adding water, NaOH, NaOH/acrylamide (AM), NaOH/AM/ammonium persulfate (APS), respectively. It can be inferred that alkalinity and the type of precursor affect the synthetic yield of ROS-HA and the highest target synthetic yield was 70.9%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optics and surface property of ROS-HA
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 1(d), the UV–Vis absorption spectra of ROS-HA showed a peak at 244 nm, corresponding to π-π* electronic transition. ROS-HA has maximum emission wavelength at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm, where emission wavelength is 387 nm (Fig. 1(e)). XRD peak of chitosan hydrogel loaded with HA was smooth and large at 20° (Fig. 1(f)). After hydrothermal carbonization, the characteristic peak of HA was retained, and peak of chitosan at 20° become rough, due to the conversion of chitosan into CDs (Fig. 1(f)). From Fig. 1(g), it can be inferred that ROS-HA was made up of chitosan, polyacrylamide and HA. 1573 cm−1 is N–H, 1449 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 is –CH2，belonging to the chitosan. 2923 cm−1 is C–H, 1215 cm−1 and 1169 cm−1 are –OH/-CO，876 cm−1 and 828 cm−1 is C3NO，belonging to polyacrylamide. The peak at 3573 cm−1, 962 cm−1 and 638 cm−1 is OH, 1100 cm−1, 1058 cm−1, 604 cm−1and 565 cm−1 is PO4 3−, belonging to HA. Fig. S1 was survey XPS spectrum of ROS-HA, it can be seen the O1s, Ca2s, Ca2p, C1s, P2s, P2p, Ca2s and Ca3p were in the figure. In C1s (Fig. 1(h)), C–C, C–O/C–N, CO accounted for 43%, 24%, 33%, respectively. In N1s (Fig. 1(i)), N–C, N–H accounted for 61%, 39%, respectively. Fig. S2 was fluorescence quantum yield of ROS-HA, the fluorescence quantum yield of ROS-HA was 9.2% by comparing the fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulfate.

The morphological investigation of ROS-HA was analyzed by HRTEM studies. Fig. 2(a) showed that synthesized ROS-HA nanorods had a length of about 60–90 nm. The TEM images Fig. 2(b) clearly showed that CDs were spherical and monodispersed on the surface of HA. The formed CDs on ROS-HA and d-spacing measured as 0.34 nm of HA and 0.24 nm of CDs (Fig. 2(c) and (d)), which confirmed the XRD d-spacing.
Optical and surface properties of ROS-HA demonstrated the binding of CDs and HA. It makes HA fluoresce in physical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coordination constant and chelation sites of ROS-CDs and ROS-HA
ce:section.ce:para: The coordination constant K of ROS scavenging carbon dots (ROS-CDs) is（1.15 ± 0.01） × 103 M−1 (Fig. 2(e)). The coordination constant K of ROS-HA is（5.68 ± 0.06） × 103 M−1 (Fig. 2(f)). The coordination constant K of ROS-HA is 4.9 times higher as much as ROS-CDs (Fig. 2(g)). Coordination constant K value of ROS-HA is much higher than that of ROS-CDs, probably because HA can bind more ions. The chelation sites of ROS-CDs were 2.89 ± 0.02 (Fig. 2(h)). The chelation sites of ROS-HA were 0.69 ± 0.02 (Fig. 2(i)). The chelation sites of ROS-CDs were 4.2 times higher as much as ROS-HA (Fig. 2(j)). The chelation sites of ROS-HA were smaller than that of ROS-CDs, which may be due to the combination of CDs and HA, resulting in a decrease in the chelation sites of ROS-HA.

Coordination constant and chelation sites proved that HA and CDs complexes were successfully realized by chelation ability. Chelation ability chemically explains the binding of CDs to HA, which in turn gives HA fluorescence properties in physical and the ability to regulate ROS in biological.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of Ca2+ detection by fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The chelation ability is utilized to enable ROS-HA to have physical fluorescence. After combination, ROS-HA still had chelation ability. In order to confirm the chelating ability after the combination, calcium ion detection experiments are carried out. In Fig. 3(a), response of ROS-HA to calcium ions was the most obvious, and the fluorescence was enhanced by 30%. Fig. 3(b) shows relative fluorescence (F/F0) spectra of calcium and ROS-HA mixed solution with interference ions. Different interference ions have little effect on the relative fluorescence spectra of ROS-HA and calcium ions. In Fig. 3(c), from 0 to 300 μM mixed calcium ions, the fluorescence of ROS-HA increased continuously. According to Fig. 3(c), two linear relationships were found by fitting integral area of the relative enhanced fluorescence intensity to concentration of calcium ions (Fig. 3(d)).

Fig. 3(e) was mechanism diagram of calcium ion detection by ROS-HA using chelation ability. ROS-CDs binded to the surface of HA using its chelating ability, and then used remaining chelating sites of ROS-CDs to recognize calcium ions. Calcium ion was detected via chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) mechanism. The CDs on the hydroxyapatite in this work have the ability to chelate. When calcium ions were added, ROS-HA chelates with calcium ions to form CDs aggregates. The Ca2+ induced CDs aggregates could reduce energy loss and improve emission efficiency of the CDs on the hydroxyapatite through non-radiative relaxation channels [40].

From Fig. 4 (a), hydroxyl radical was removed with the increase of ROS-HA concentration. The clearance rates at 1.2 mg/mL concentrations were 71.80% (Fig. 4(b)). The OH- inhibition IC50 of ROS-HA was 0.59 mg/mL. From Fig. 4(c), superoxide radical was removed with increase of ROS-HA concentration. The clearance rates at 3.6 mg/mL concentrations were 59.91% (Fig. 4(d)). The superoxide radical inhibition IC50 of ROS-HA was 2.1 mg/mL. Fig. 4(e) demonstrated that with increase of the concentration of ROS-HA, DPPH radical was continuously scavenged, and maximum scavenging rate was reached at 1 mg/mL. The IC50 was 0.4 mg/mL from Fig. 4(f).

Fig. 4(g) and (h) observe the oxidation-reduction effect of CDs in ROS-HA. ROS-HA hinder the electron transfer between Fe3+ ions and electrode. In Fig. 4(g), it is found ROS-CDs had the ability to hinder the electron transfer and HA didn't. Fig. 4(h) was a redox reaction at the electrode surface with the 0.20 mg/mL ROS-HA having strongest peak. This indicates that the charge transfer ability of ROS-HA is related to the surface CDs on the ROS-HA.

ROS scavenging ability of ROS-HA proves that ROS-HA has ROS scavenging ability in biological, which is not available in HA. The ROS scavenging ability is conferred on HA by the chelating ability of CDs.

In Fig. 4(i), the max adsorption capacity is 45.3 μg BSA/mg ROS-HA. BSA is a protein model drug. In current literatures, CDs do not have ability to adsorb protein drugs, while hydroxyapatite has ability to adsorb BSA. According to BSA adsorption experiments, it was proved that ROS-HA has the potential to load protein drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS-HA enhancing hydrogel strength, modulus and adhesion
ce:section.ce:para: HA enhance adhesion of hydrogels [41,42] and CDs can enhance mechanical properties of hydrogels [43–45]. Here we add ROS-HA to the poly (acrylic acid)-N-hydroxysuccinimide ester (PAA-NHS ester) chitosan hydrogel (Fig. 5 (a)). The NHS ester groups grafted on the poly (acrylic acid) can couple covalently to primary amine groups on different tissues quickly. In Fig. 5(a), the picture on the right is the photo of the hydrogel under white light and ultraviolet light. It can be found that the hydrogel has fluorescence after adding ROS-HA.

To characterize the compatibility of ROS-HA with hydrogel, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and elemental mapping analysis were performed. Fig. 5(b) was SEM image of the hydrogel with ROS-HA and Fig. 5(c) was elemental mapping analysis of Ca element. Fig. 5(d) was merged SEM image with Ca distribution maps. Calcium element was derived from hydroxyapatite in ROS-HA and calcium element uniformly dispersed in polyacrylic acid hydrogels. The yellow color represents the location of calcium element from Fig. 5(c) and (d), and the location of Ca element can demonstrate the uniform fraction of ROS-HA in the polyacrylic acid hydrogel. The P element from hydroxyapatite of ROS-HA in Fig. 5(e) also demonstrated the uniform fraction of ROS-HA in hydrogel. ROS-HA had good compatibility with polyacrylic acid hydrogels according to the microstructure analysis and enhancement the mechanical properties of hydrogel.

Fig. 5(f) demonstrated 10% ROS-HA (wt%) hydrogel composites had highest tensile stress at break. In Fig. 5(g), Young's modulus of hydrogel composites increased with the increase of ROS-HA content. In Figs. 5(h), 10% ROS-HA (wt%) hydrogel composites had the best adhesion performance and had 86% increase compared to the hydrogel without ROS-HA. The max tensile strain in Fig. 5(i) of the 0%, 5%, 10%, 15% ROS-HA hydrogel composites were 1407%, 1394%, 1155%, 264%, respectively. The swelling ratio in Fig. 5(j) of the 0%, 5%, 10%, 15% ROS-HA hydrogel composites were 714%, 585%, 547%, 491%, respectively.

The addition of ROS-HA improves the strength, modulus and adhesion performance of the hydrogel composites. In swelling experiment, with the increase of ROS-HA content, the swelling degree of the hydrogel decreases continuously, which represents the continuous improvement of the cross-linking degree of the hydrogel composites. Therefore, the hydrogel composites become denser and water was not easy to enter.

The incorporation of ROS-HA improved the strength, modulus and adhesion properties of the polyacrylic acid hydrogel composites. The CDs on the ROS-HA were assembled on the surface of the HA using the chelating ability. After the ROS-CDs chelated the surface of HA, it still had the ability of chelating calcium ions. The coordination constant K of ROS-HA was（5.68 ± 0.06） × 103 M−1 and chelation sites of ROS-HA was 0.69 ± 0.02. The maximum hydroxyl radical scavenging rate and the maximum superoxide radical scavenging rate of ROS-HA were 72% and 60%, respectively. The adsorption capacity of ROS-HA on BSA protein model drugs was 45.3 μg BSA/mg ROS-HA. ROS-HA hydrogel composites had 86% increase in adhesion performance compared to the hydrogel without ROS-HA. ROS-HA could endow hydrogel with ROS scavenging ability and stronger adhesion, so the hydrogel is expected to be used as a tissue band-aid and to lower reactive oxygen species in wounds at the same time.

Guanxiong Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Baoqiang Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Funding acquisition. Zonglin Liu: Data curation, Methodology. Jiaxin Dong: Data curation, Methodology. Xuefeng Kang: Conceptualization. Yujie Feng: Validation. Dechang Jia: Validation. Yu Zhou: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Science Foundation of China (52061135204, 51972086), Russian Science Foundation (21-43-00020), Heilongjiang Provincial Key R&D projects (2022ZX02C24), Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Russian Federation (075-15-2022-1114), Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University) Ministry of Education (SWZ-ZD202103), State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment of Harbin Institute of Technology (2022TS21), Interdisciplinary Research Foundation of HIT (IR2021105), China Scholarship Council (202006120197).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.coco.2022.101488.
